🤝 Contributing to Open Source

Latest · Community-maintained · Guide

Contributing to open source projects is one of the best ways to learn, grow, and give back to the developer community. This guide covers everything you need to know to make meaningful contributions.

Before You Start

  • Read the README: Understand what the project does and how to set it up
  • Check CONTRIBUTING.md: Look for project-specific contribution guidelines
  • Review open issues: Find good first issue or help wanted labels
  • Join the community: Introduce yourself on Discord, Slack, or mailing lists

Making Your First Contribution

  1. Fork the repository to your account
  2. Clone your fork locally
  3. Create a feature branch from main
  4. Make your changes with clear, atomic commits
  5. Write or update tests for your changes
  6. Push to your fork and open a pull request
# Fork and clone
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/project.git
cd project

# Create a feature branch
git checkout -b fix/typo-in-readme

# Make changes, then commit
git add -A
git commit -m "docs: fix typo in installation section"

# Push and open a PR
git push origin fix/typo-in-readme

Writing Good Commit Messages

Follow the Conventional Commits specification:

# Format
<type>(<scope>): <description>

# Examples
feat(auth): add OAuth2 support for third-party login
fix(parser): handle edge case with empty input arrays
docs(readme): update installation instructions for v2
chore(deps): bump lodash from 4.17.20 to 4.17.21
test(api): add integration tests for user endpoints

Code Review Etiquette

Code reviews are a collaborative process. Whether you're a reviewer or contributor:

  • Be respectful and constructive in all feedback
  • Focus on the code, not the person
  • Explain the "why" behind requested changes
  • Respond to reviews promptly
  • Use suggestions for small inline fixes
  • Approve when satisfied — don't block on nitpicks

Types of Contributions

Code isn't the only way to contribute:

  • Documentation: Fix typos, improve guides, add examples
  • Bug reports: File detailed issues with reproduction steps
  • Design: UI/UX improvements, logos, branding
  • Testing: Write tests, report edge cases, fuzzing
  • Translation: Help localise the project
  • Mentoring: Help newcomers get started

Maintainer Tips

  • Use issue and PR templates to standardise submissions
  • Label issues clearly (bug, enhancement, good first issue)
  • Set up automated CI checks for all pull requests
  • Document your release process and branching strategy
  • Recognise contributors in your README or CONTRIBUTORS file
  • Be responsive — stale PRs discourage future contributions
